name: Mi'kmaq
traditionalTerritories: Mi'kma'ki — the ancestral and unceded homeland of the Mi'kmaq people, comprising present-day Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Island, New Brunswick, the Gaspé Peninsula of Quebec, Cape Breton Island, and the island of Newfoundland. Also includes parts of Maine. One of the most geographically extensive coastal First Nations territories in eastern Canada.
culturalNotes: The Mi'kmaq (meaning 'my kin-friends') are one of the original peoples of the Atlantic Maritime region. They were among the first Indigenous peoples to have sustained contact with European explorers (15th-16th centuries) and have one of the longest documented histories of Indigenous-European relations in North America. Mi'kmaq traditional culture is oriented around the maritime environment — the sea, rivers, and coastal forests — rather than the inland boreal forest of many Cree and Anishinaabe peoples. The Mi'kmaq Nation is organized into seven historic districts (Kespukwitk, Sipekne'katik, Eskikewa'kik, Unama'kik, Epekwitk aq Piktuk, Siknikt, Kespek). Mi'kmaq communities are among the most vocal advocates for treaty rights in Atlantic Canada.
heritageSkillRelevance: Birchbark canoe construction — Mi'kmaq canoe styles are distinct from Great Lakes designs, optimized for coastal and open-water travel
Sweetgrass basket weaving — Mi'kmaq sweetgrass baskets are internationally recognized as a distinct art form
Porcupine quillwork and later beadwork on birchbark
Coastal fishing — lobster trapping (traditional), salmon fishing, eel harvesting
Birchbark containers and wigwam (the dome shelter with specific Mi'kmaq construction variations)
Smoked and dried fish preparation (eel, salmon, herring)
Harvesting coastal edibles — dulse (seaweed), fiddleheads, wild blueberries
Moose hide preparation and traditional footwear (moccasins)
communityAuthorizedResources: Mi'kmaw Native Friendship Centre — mnfc.ca — Halifax, Nova Scotia
Nova Scotia Mi'kmaq Cultural Programs through the Assembly of Nova Scotia Mi'kmaw Chiefs
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Sweetgrass basket weaving and canoe construction are Level 1 with Mi'kmaq attribution. Coastal fishing methods (general techniques) are Level 1. Ceremonial uses of sweetgrass and sacred plants are Level 2-3. Traditional medicine practices are Level 3.
